text: Hello and welcome to this new episode of This is My Architecture. I am Sebastian and I'm joined
today by Mohamed Sleek from RMX. Hello Mohamed. Hello Sebastian thank you thank you for having
me. So can you tell us a little bit more about RMX Sleek please? Sure, RMX is a comprehensive
logistics and a transportation solutions provider. We operate in 65 countries and we have 15,000
employees globally. The company moves products everything from heavy industrial freight into
international cross-border e-commerce and domestic fulfillment as well and we have over 250 distribution
centers across the world. Okay and can you tell us a bit more on the challenges you are going through
and how you are planning to solve them? Of course so one of the main challenges that the industry is
facing today specifically in our emerging markets is a lack of a proper address management structure
within our regions as compared to some of the mature markets where there's reliance on postal codes and
zip codes to for the drivers to navigate their addresses and to navigate their deliveries. So
for us there's a lot of dependency on descriptive addresses so we really wanted to deploy a generative
machine learning algorithm or a text matching algorithm that looks at an address in reference
to historical data and come up with an x y coordinate to reduce dependency on a skilled worker that looks at
an address and tries to sort it and you know to reduce the number of calls that we make for customers to
acquire addresses and to improve customer experience at the end of the day. Okay so can you walk us through
this architecture please? Sure so the solution has two main flows. The first flow is really about
advanced analytics and machine learning capabilities to build our machine learning models and the second one is
really about empowering our data scientists for the end user flow. So the first element we use Amazon glue to
move large amounts of data from our data sources and as an ingestion service into Redshift and we store three
months of data in Redshift for us to for the data scientists to build their models but also we use S3 to
ingest large large amounts of data sets and in this scenario a lot of address data into S3 which is really a scalable efficient data layer.
A centralized data lake where we ingest all of these data using Amazon glue and we also have another stream where we use
where we move reference data for our data scientists to be able to you know run rapid
some some references on on on rapid access to the data runs run their tests to make sure that they optimize their
machine learning models and build their predictive models. Okay so you mentioned so you're only keeping
three months of data on the Redshift so can you explain us a little bit more why and
the specific processing you're doing on it? Sure so so it really helps our data scientists the three months
of data for for data exploration and data preparation and also some of the data transformation that we
needed to do to transform it from raw raw data into data that is used for accurate prediction to build
accurate prediction models. So anything from profiling from data cleansing and data transformation to be able to
build proper machine learning models and then the next step really is for us to build and optimize the model using SageMaker.
So the building of the model it fetches data so SageMaker fetches data from Redshift to build and operate
these model and optimize them and in order for us to train these models to become a bit more accurate

we use Redshift spectrum to fetch the data from S3 to make these models a bit more more accurate. So the minute the precision of these models are accurate enough to drive business outcomes we are able to deploy these models
onto our operating systems and driver apps and all the front end applications to consume SageMaker

to consume SageMaker end deployments using a serverless approach through the Amazon API Gateway and Amazon Lambda to consume these models from Amazon SageMaker that is predicting accurate addresses and accurate x y coordinates and this happens in a matter of seconds so we can deploy and we can add value to the business on the ground.
Okay so I understand that SageMaker is one of the key components we have in architecture so any specific reason why you're using SageMaker?

Of course so data scientists need to use tools very advanced tools to deploy their models and to drive business outcomes very quick and very fast and typically we're looking for a machine learning platform that has all of these components in a managed services manner.

I think SageMaker offers the capabilities to build to develop and to train or even deploy our models all under one platform so that we can maximize value very quick and be able to add value to the business as quick as possible and speed to value to market becomes very relevant in this scenario.
Okay and then so what was the main benefit so besides so all the capabilities from SageMaker so any business benefits out of this architecture?

So imagine when we when the shipments start from a fulfillment journey very early on when we create the shipment in our landscape using Amazon SageMaker and using these training models and API models we are able to geocode our addresses very early on in the fulfillment journey of the shipment so we can optimize our last mile capacity and optimize our routes and also we also use it to plan capacity very early on in the journey so we've seen a 10% improvement on our average

success rates on our success rates on our success rates on our daily basis in our emerging markets and core markets because we're improving the quality of the addresses and we're using advanced tools to navigate and empower the driver to navigate even better and optimize the routes and this is huge for us so we moved so that we improved the productivity of the driver by almost 10% in these core markets.
Okay that's great so thanks Jake for working us through your architecture.
